shot.subject: field
shot.notes: the meadow in full sun; drifts of color scattered through the green

segments.text: The grass is the garden's economy. The trees are its architecture. But this — this is its advertising district.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: glide low across a drift of blooms, colors strobing past the lens

segments.text: Nothing else in the garden spends like a flower spends. Pigment, scent, geometry, nectar — all of it lavished on a single message, broadcast on every channel at once: come here.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: one bloom fills the frame, petals radiating from a glowing center

segments.text: The flowers.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: dead-center overhead view; the petal spiral in perfect symmetry

segments.text: Look straight down into a bloom and count. Three petals. Five. Eight. Thirteen. Almost never four; almost never seven. The flowers of this garden keep returning to the same strange menu of numbers — and it is no accident of the Gardener's taste.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: macro spiraling slowly around the flower's center

segments.text: These are the numbers of growth itself — each term the sum of the two before, the arithmetic a growing tip produces when it packs new petals into the space the old ones left. The spiral is not decoration. It is the construction method, left visible.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a quick montage: several species, each a different count and color

segments.text: So every bloom in the meadow is a small theorem with a colored proof. The bees, of course, have never checked the math. They simply land on it.

shot.subject: bee
shot.notes: a bumblebee barrels in and lands heavily; the bloom dips and sways

segments.text: Because all of this — every petal, every pigment — exists for an audience. A flower is a broadcast aimed at six-legged customers, and its shape is a landing deck rated for their weight and their clumsiness.

shot.subject: bee
shot.notes: macro: the bee working the flower's center, fur golden with pollen

segments.text: The transaction never varies. Nectar for the visitor; pollen on the visitor. The flower pays its couriers in sugar, and its mail is delivered flower to flower on fur and wing. We toured this contract from the bee's side. From the flower's side, it is even bolder — the plant has outsourced its own reproduction, and pays only in syrup.

shot.subject: butterfly
shot.notes: a butterfly drifts down onto a neighboring bloom, wings breathing

segments.text: The butterflies work the same district on lighter contracts. Less freight, more range. A meadow in season runs a full courier service, and every route is paid in color.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: clusters of blooms drifted through the grass, clearly grouped, not random

segments.text: Notice how the flowers gather — drifts here, singletons there, colonies where the ground suits them. Some of that is the way of seeds. And some of it is the other thing, the thing this garden never quite hides.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: slow orbit of one exceptional bloom, its form almost too deliberate

segments.text: These flowers are designed. Somewhere beyond the sky, the Gardener composes them — petal count, curl and flare, color and variation — the way other gardeners breed roses. Each variety in this field is a decision. The meadow is a portfolio.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: from the soil, looking up through backlit petals like stained glass

segments.text: The bees cannot tell bred beauty from wild. Sunlight through a petal is sunlight through a petal. Whatever the flowers' origin, the light treats them all as native.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: macro dissection by camera: petal, then center, then stem in turn

segments.text: Take one bloom apart, and every component confesses its job. The petals: pure signage, colored on the outward face where the customers fly, cheap and disposable by design. The center: the actual shop — pollen and nectar, inventory and till. The stem: a mast engineered like the grass towers, but sized for a heavier, gaudier cargo.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: the petal's surface at extreme magnification: gradients, sheen, depth of color

segments.text: Even the color is engineered in layers. A petal is not painted; it is built — pigment below, structure above, the surface itself sculpted to catch light at the angles a descending bee will see. Some of these signals are tuned for eyes that read colors we cannot. The meadow is broadcasting on channels we can only guess at.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: wind sweeps the drift; every bloom noses into the moving air and recovers

segments.text: And the whole advertisement is mounted on a gimbal. Watch the drift take a gust: each flower swings, sheds the load, and swims back upright — the grass's philosophy of bending, fitted with a billboard. A rigid flower would be confetti by noon. A flexible one simply nods to the weather and reopens the shop.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a bloom tracked over a full day in seconds: opening, following light, closing

segments.text: Compress a day into moments, and the final mechanism appears: the slow choreography of open and close. Dawn unfurls the signage; dusk retracts it, folding the inventory away from the cold and the pointless dark. Nothing about a flower stands still. It only moves too slowly, and too politely, to be caught doing it.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: late golden light sweeps the meadow; blooms flare and dim as it passes

segments.text: For all their spending, flowers keep the shortest books in the garden. The grass endures. The trees accumulate. A bloom simply spends — everything, gloriously, now.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: petals trembling in evening wind, edges translucent

segments.text: It is the garden's one true extravagance, and its most honest one. Nothing that burns this brightly is saving for winter.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: dusk; the colors gutter out one drift at a time as light fails

segments.text: And when the light goes, the advertising district closes with it — to reopen at dawn, full price, as if the night had never happened.

shot.subject: tree
shot.notes: rise from the meadow floor up a dark trunk into spreading branches

segments.text: From the garden's brightest spenders, we turn to its oldest institutions — the only residents that grow taller than the grass, carry whole neighborhoods in their arms, and occasionally, thunderously, fall.

shot.subject: tree
shot.notes: the treeline at evening, crowns black against the sky

segments.text: Next: the trees.
